title_en: Discourse on Diagnosing Disease by the Cubit Skin
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: "I wish to diagnose illness without observing complexion or holding the pulse, relying solely on examining the cubit skin (chi fu — the skin of the forearm between wrist and elbow), so as to know what lies within the body from what appears without. How is this done?" Qibo replied: "By examining whether the cubit skin is slack or tense, small or large, smooth or rough, and whether the flesh beneath it is firm or frail, the pattern of the disease can be determined."

When one observes a person whose eye sockets are slightly swollen, as though he had just risen from sleep, whose neck vessel throbs visibly, who coughs from time to time, and when the hands and feet are pressed the depression left behind does not spring back — this is wind-water skin distension.

If the cubit skin is smooth and moist, this is wind. If the flesh of the cubit is weak and frail, this is jieyi — a languor disorder marked by generalized weakness, lassitude, and wasting; if the patient lies quietly at ease and yet the flesh wastes away, this is a cold-heat disorder, and it cannot be treated. If the cubit skin is smooth and glossy as though oiled, this is wind. If the cubit skin is rough, this is wind impediment. If the cubit skin is coarse like the scales of a withered fish, this is water-overflow with fluid retention. If the cubit skin is very hot and the pulse is full and restless, this is warm disease; if the pulse is full and also slippery, the disease is about to break out. If the cubit skin is cold and the pulse is small, this is diarrhea with depleted qi. If the cubit skin burns like a torch, hot at first and turning cold afterward, this is a cold-heat disorder; if the cubit skin is cold at first, and after being held for a long while grows warm, this too is a cold-heat disorder.

If heat is localized to the elbow alone, there is heat above the lower back; if heat is localized to the hand alone, there is heat below the lower back. If heat is localized in front of the elbow, there is heat in the chest; if heat is localized behind the elbow, there is heat in the shoulder and back. If heat is localized in the mid-forearm, there is heat in the lower back and abdomen; if heat is felt three or four inches below the coarse skin behind the elbow, there are parasites in the intestines. If the palm is hot, there is heat in the abdomen; if the palm is cold, there is cold in the abdomen. If, on the white flesh above the thenar eminence (the "fish" region at the base of the thumb), greenish blood vessels appear, there is cold in the stomach.

If the cubit skin burns like a torch and the Renying pulse is large, there ought to be blood loss; if the cubit skin is hard and large while the pulse is very small, the qi depleted, and the oppressive distress increases, the patient will die at once.

If the eyes show a red color, the disease is in the heart; white, in the lung; green-blue, in the liver; yellow, in the spleen; black, in the kidney. If the yellow color cannot be clearly named, the disease is in the chest.

In diagnosing eye pain: if a red vessel runs from above downward, this is Taiyang disease; if it runs from below upward, this is Yangming disease; if it runs from the outer canthus toward the inner, this is Shaoyang disease.

In diagnosing cold-heat disorders, when a red vessel extends up and down as far as the pupil: if one such vessel is seen, the patient will die within a year; if one and a half vessels are seen, within a year and a half; if two vessels, within two years; if two and a half vessels, within two years and a half; if three vessels, within three years.

In diagnosing toothache from caries, press along the course of the yang channels as they arrive: wherever there is excess, heat is localized there alone — if on the left, the left is hot; if on the right, the right is hot; if above, the upper part is hot; if below, the lower part is hot.

In diagnosing the blood vessels: much redness indicates much heat; much green-blue indicates much pain; much black indicates a longstanding impediment disorder; if redness, black, and green-blue all appear together, this is a cold-heat disorder.

When the body aches and the complexion is faintly yellow, the teeth are coated with a yellow film, and the nails are yellow, this is jaundice. When the patient lies quietly at ease, the urine is dark yellow-red, and the pulse is small and rough, the patient has no appetite.
When a person is ill, if the pulse at the Cunkou is equal in size to the pulse at Renying, and equal also in depth, whether floating or sunken, the disease will be difficult to resolve.
In a woman, if the pulse of the Hand Shaoyin channel beats vigorously, she is pregnant.

When an infant is ill, if all the hair on the head stands up reversed, the infant will certainly die. If a green-blue vessel rises up between the ears, this indicates pulling pain. If the stool contains red flecks together with diarrhea of undigested food, and the pulse is small while the hands and feet are cold, this is difficult to resolve; if there is diarrhea of undigested food with a small pulse but the hands and feet are warm, the diarrhea is easily resolved.

In the transformations of the four seasons and the ascendancy of cold and heat, doubled yin must turn into yang, and doubled yang must turn into yin. Thus yin governs cold and yang governs heat; hence when cold reaches its extreme it turns to heat, and when heat reaches its extreme it turns to cold. Thus it is said: cold gives rise to heat, and heat gives rise to cold — this is the transformation of yin and yang.

Thus it is said: if one is injured by cold in winter, in spring one will develop warm disease; if injured by wind in spring, in summer one will develop diarrhea with undigested food and dysenteric disorders; if injured by summer-heat in summer, in autumn one will develop malarial fevers; if injured by dampness in autumn, in winter one will develop cough. This is called the sequence of the four seasons.

title_en: Discourse on the Categories of Needling, and on True and Evil Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: "I have heard that there are five guarding regimens of needling — what are they?" Qibo replied: "Indeed there are five guarding regimens: the first is called 'Shaking Off the Dust' (zhen'ai); the second, 'Dispelling the Blindness' (fameng); the third, 'Removing the Nails' (quzhao); the fourth, 'Stripping Off the Robe' (chexi); and the fifth, 'Resolving Confusion' (jiehuo)." Huangdi said: "You speak of these five guarding regimens, Master, but I do not yet understand their meaning." Qibo said: "'Shaking Off the Dust' means needling the outer channels to remove yang disease; 'Dispelling the Blindness' means needling the transport points of the bowels to remove disease of the bowels; 'Removing the Nails' means needling the joints and the limb network vessels; 'Stripping Off the Robe' means needling all of the unusual transport points of the yang channels; 'Resolving Confusion' means fully understanding how to regulate yin and yang, to supplement and drain what is in excess or deficient, and to shift the balance between them."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Shaking Off the Dust,' yet you explain it as needling the outer channels to remove yang disease — I do not understand what this means. I wish to hear it in full." Qibo said: "In 'Shaking Off the Dust,' the yang qi surges greatly in reversal, filling and swelling the chest; the patient heaves his shoulders in labored breathing, the great qi rebels upward, he pants and gasps, sitting slumped forward, and finds even the smell of dust or smoke intolerable; he cannot eat without becoming breathless. Let me speak of 'Shaking Off the Dust' — its effect is swifter even than the shaking off of dust." Huangdi said: "Good. How is it applied?" Qibo said: "It is applied at Tianrong." Huangdi said: "And for one who coughs, whose qi rebels upward, who is exhausted and clumsy in movement, and who suffers chest pain, how is it applied?" Qibo said: "It is applied at Lianquan." Huangdi said: "Is there a measure to observe in applying it?" Qibo said: "In needling Tianrong, one should not exceed one li in depth; in needling Lianquan, one stops once the blood changes in color." Huangdi said: "Excellent."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Dispelling the Blindness,' but I still do not grasp its meaning. Now 'dispelling the blindness' concerns one whose ears can hear nothing and whose eyes can see nothing — yet you explain it as needling the transport points of the bowels to remove disease of the bowels. Through which transport point is this accomplished? I wish to hear the reason." Qibo said: "How subtle a question this is! This is the great essence of needling, the utmost of the needling art, belonging to the realm of spiritual brilliance — something that spoken word or written text still cannot fully convey. Let me speak of 'Dispelling the Blindness' — its effect is swifter even than the dispelling of blindness." Huangdi said: "Good. I wish to hear it in full." Qibo said: "In needling for this, one must do so at midday, needling Tinggong, aiming so as to reach the pupil, so that a sound is heard within the ear — this is the transport point." Huangdi said: "Good. What is meant by 'a sound heard within the ear'?" Qibo said: "In needling this pattern, one presses firmly with the hand upon both nostrils, and then has the patient abruptly hold his breath and cry out — the response will surely be felt through the needle." Huangdi said: "Good. This is what is called 'acting upon what cannot be seen' — needling without visual observation, yet grasping the disease as though it were seen, so that spirit and brightness meet one another."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Removing the Nails,' yet you explain it as needling the joints and the limb network vessels. I wish to hear it in full." Qibo said: "The lower back and spine are the great joints of the body; the limbs and shins are the channels by which a person walks and moves freely; the penis and scrotum are the pivot within the body, the sign of yin essence, and the pathway of the fluids. Thus when eating and drinking are not moderated, and joy and anger arise at improper times, the fluids overflow within and descend to lodge in the testicles; the pathway of blood becomes obstructed, and the swelling grows larger day by day without ceasing, so that bending and stretching become difficult and walking becomes impossible. In this disease, water gathers glistening within, neither rising nor descending; it is treated with the pi-needle or the stone lancet. Its form cannot be hidden, and it is always exposed and cannot be concealed — hence it is called 'Removing the Nails.'" Huangdi said: "Good."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Stripping Off the Robe,' yet you explain it as needling all of the unusual transport points of the yang channels, which have no fixed location. I wish to hear it in full." Qibo said: "This concerns a case in which the yang qi is in excess while the yin qi is insufficient. When yin qi is insufficient, heat arises within; when yang qi is in excess, heat arises without. When internal and external heat contend with one another, the patient feels hotter than if embracing burning charcoal; outwardly he dreads the touch even of silk or cotton cloth, unable to bear it near his body, nor even near his sleeping mat. The pores become blocked and closed, so that sweat cannot be released; the tongue becomes scorched and the lips wither, drying out further day by day, and the patient no longer distinguishes good food from bad in eating and drinking." Huangdi said: "Good. How is this treated?" Qibo said: "One needles Tianfu and Dazhu, three punctures each, and further needles Zhonglü, in order to remove the heat; one supplements the Foot and Hand Taiyin channels, in order to remove the sweating. Once the heat is removed and the sweating diminishes, the effect is swifter than the stripping off of a robe." Huangdi said: "Good."

Huangdi said: "You spoke of the guarding regimen called 'Resolving Confusion,' yet you explain it as fully understanding how to regulate yin and yang, to supplement and drain what is in excess or deficient, and to shift the balance between them. How, then, is confusion actually resolved?" Qibo said: "When great wind evil lodges in the body, the blood vessels become unevenly deficient on one side — where deficient, there is insufficiency, and where replete, there is excess. The body loses its balance between light and heavy, tilting and slumping to one side; the patient no longer knows east from west, nor north from south; he is now up, now down, now turned over, now turned back, tumbling about without constancy, worse even than ordinary confusion." Huangdi said: "Good. How is this treated?" Qibo said: "One drains what is in excess and supplements what is deficient, so that yin and yang are restored to balance. When the needle is used in this way, its effect is swifter than the resolving of confusion." Huangdi said: "Good. Let this be stored in the chamber of the Numinous Orchid, and I dare not let it be recklessly disclosed."

Huangdi said: "I have heard that there are five evils treated by needling — what are the five evils?" Qibo said: "There is the disease that carries welling-abscess, the disease of excessive fullness, the disease of narrowness and smallness, the disease of heat, and the disease of cold — these are called the five evils." Huangdi said: "How does one needle the five evils?" Qibo said: "In needling the five evils, there are no more than five methods: intense heat is made to dissolve and vanish; swelling and accumulation are made to disperse and disappear; cold impediment is warmed further; smallness is supplemented with yang; and largeness must be removed. Let me set forth these methods."

In needling welling-abscess evil in general, do not confront it head-on while it is at its height; alter its habitual course and shift its nature. If pus has not yet formed, the fragile pathway can still be redirected; drive the evil from its lodging place, so that, finding no place to settle, it disperses and vanishes. Wherever excess occurs along the yin or yang channels as a welling-abscess, needle its transport point and drain it.

In needling great evil in general, make it smaller day by day: drain and dislodge its excess, and thereby increase its deficiency. Strike swiftly at its open pathway, needling the evil itself; observe closely the flesh and muscle, taking care not to go against what is true and proper. Needle between the flesh and muscle of the yang channels.

In needling small evil in general, make it larger day by day: supplement its deficiency, and thereby there will be no harm. Observe where it lies, and meet it at its boundary, so that, whether near or far, the qi fully arrives; when the evil can no longer invade from without, it exhausts itself of its own accord. Needle between the flesh and muscle.

In needling heat evil in general, disperse it outward until it pales and fades away, letting it wander off and never return, and thereby there will be no more disease. Open up a passage, throw open the gate, so that the evil may find its way out — then the disease will be cured.

In needling cold evil in general, make it warm day by day: insert and withdraw the needle slowly, so as to summon the spirit qi. Once the gate is closed, the qi does not scatter; deficiency and excess become regulated, and the qi is thereby preserved.

Huangdi said: "What needles are properly used for these?" Qibo said: "In needling welling-abscess, use the pi-needle, the sword-shaped needle; in needling the great excess, use the sharp-pointed needle; in needling the small deficiency, use the round-sharp needle; in needling heat, use the caizhen, a slender tapering needle; in needling cold, use the haozhen, the fine, hair-like needle."

Let me now speak of an explanatory discourse: the human body corresponds to Heaven and Earth, and accords with the four seasons; the human being takes part with Heaven and Earth, and so this correspondence can be explained. Where the ground below is damp and marshy, reeds and cattails grow above it — this is how one may know the relative abundance of a person's physical form and qi. Yin and yang correspond to cold and heat: when it is hot, moistening rains fall from above, and the roots and stalks of plants hold little sap; in the human being, the qi lies at the exterior, the skin is relaxed, the pores are open, the blood and qi thin out at the surface, sweat is greatly released, and the skin is moist and glossy. When it is cold, the earth freezes and the water turns to ice; in the human being, the qi lies within, the skin becomes dense, the pores are closed, sweat does not come out, the blood and qi are strong and concentrated within, and the flesh is firm and rough. At such a time, even one skilled at conducting water cannot make it flow through ice; even one skilled at digging through earth cannot break through the frozen ground; and even one skilled in using the needle cannot immediately relieve reversal disorder of the four limbs when the blood vessels are congealed and bound, firm and pressed together, no longer flowing back and forth — this too cannot at once be softened. Thus one who would conduct water must wait for the weather to turn warm, so that the ice melts and the frozen ground thaws, before the water can flow and the earth can be dug. The human pulse and vessels are just the same. In treating reversal disorders, one must first apply warming compresses to harmonize the channels — the palms and armpits, the elbows and legs, the nape and spine — in order to bring them into balance; once the fire qi has penetrated through, the blood and vessels then begin to flow. Only afterward does one examine the disease: if the pulse is moist and glossy, one needles it to bring it to balance; if it is hard and tight, one breaks through it to disperse it, stopping only once the qi has descended — this is what is called 'releasing the knot.'

The general method of using the needle lies in regulating the qi. Qi accumulates in the stomach, from which nutritive qi and defensive qi are made to flow freely, each along its own pathway. Ancestral qi (zongqi — the qi formed from the union of respired air and the qi of grain, which gathers in the chest) collects in the Sea of Qi; that which descends flows into the Qi Thoroughfare (qijie), and that which ascends travels along the pathway of respiration. Thus when reversal cold occurs in the feet, it is because the ancestral qi fails to descend, and the blood within the vessels congeals and stagnates; if one does not use warming to regulate it, one cannot resolve the condition.

One who uses the needle must first examine whether the channels and network vessels are excess or deficient, palpating along their course, pressing and plucking at them, observing where they respond and move — only then does one select the point and needle it. When the six channels are all in balance, this is called 'without disease'; even if disease is present, it is called one that resolves of itself. If, in a single channel, the upper part is excess and the lower part deficient so that the flow is obstructed, this must be because a transverse network vessel, engorged and full, presses upon the great channel, causing the obstruction; one observes this and drains it — this too is what is called 'releasing the knot.'

When there is cold above and heat below, first needle the Taiyang points at the nape, retaining the needle for a long while; once needling is complete, apply warming compresses to the nape and shoulder blades, allowing the heat to descend and merge with the lower region, stopping only then — this is what is called 'pushing it upward.' When there is heat above and cold below, observe the deficient vessel that has sunk into the channels and network vessels, and needle it, stopping only once the qi has descended — this is what is called 'drawing it downward.'

When great heat pervades the whole body, and the patient is manic, seeing, hearing, and speaking of things that are not real, one examines the Foot Yangming channel and its great network vessel and needles them; if deficient, one supplements it, and where there is stagnant blood with excess, one drains it. One has the patient lie face-up, and, standing at his head, presses the pulsating vessels of the neck firmly with the four fingers of both hands, holding them for a long while, kneading and pinching along them, pushing downward as far as the supraclavicular fossa, and then, stopping and beginning again as before, continues until the heat is dispelled, only then stopping — this is what is called 'pushing it out to disperse it.'

Huangdi said: "There are cases where a single vessel gives rise to dozens of different diseases — sometimes pain, sometimes welling-abscess, sometimes heat, sometimes cold, sometimes itching, sometimes impediment, sometimes numbness — the transformations are endless. What is the reason for this?" Qibo said: "All of these are produced by evil qi." Huangdi said: "I have heard that there is true qi, upright qi, and evil qi. What is meant by true qi?" Qibo said: "True qi is that which is received from Heaven; it combines with the qi of grain to fill and nourish the body. Upright qi (zhengqi) is upright wind — wind that comes from a single direction, being neither a replete wind nor a vacuous, thief-like wind. Evil qi is the thieving harm done to a person by vacuous, thief-like wind; when it strikes a person, it penetrates deeply and cannot leave of its own accord. Upright wind, when it strikes a person, penetrates only shallowly; it combines with the body's own qi and departs of its own accord, for its qi arrives weak and soft, unable to overcome the true qi, and so it departs by itself."

When vacuous, thief-like evil wind strikes a person, it makes him shiver and his body tremble, raising the fine body hair and opening the pores. If it penetrates deeply and, within, strikes against the bone, it becomes bone impediment; if it strikes against the sinews, it becomes sinew contracture; if it strikes within the vessels, it becomes blood stasis, and where blood fails to flow freely, it becomes welling-abscess. If it strikes the flesh and contends with the defensive qi, then, where yang prevails, it becomes heat, and where yin prevails, it becomes cold. When it becomes cold, the true qi departs; once departed, there is deficiency, and in deficiency, cold lodges between the skin and flesh. When this qi is expelled outward, the pores open, the fine body hair stirs, and the qi moves back and forth — this produces itching. If it lingers and does not depart, it becomes impediment. If the defensive qi fails to circulate, it becomes numbness.

When vacuous, thief-like evil wind lodges unevenly in one half of the body, penetrating deeply and settling within among the nutritive and defensive qi, then, as the nutritive and defensive qi gradually decline, the true qi departs, and the evil qi alone remains, giving rise to hemiplegia. Where the evil qi lies more shallowly, there is pain confined to one side of the vessel.

When vacuous, thief-like evil wind penetrates deeply into the body, cold and heat contend with one another, lingering long and becoming fixed within. Where cold prevails over heat, the bones ache and the flesh withers; where heat prevails over cold, the flesh rots and the muscle decays into pus, and if this injures the bone within, the injury to the bone within becomes bone-corrosion. Where there is a preexisting affliction of the sinews in front, so that the sinews are bent and cannot be extended, and the evil qi lodges between them without dispersing, this gives rise to sinew-tumor (jinliu). Where there is a binding-up, and qi gathers there, and defensive qi lodges there without returning, so that fluids remain long in that place and combine with the stagnant qi, this becomes intestinal-tumor (changliu) — a condition that, if longstanding, takes several years to form, and which feels soft when pressed with the hand. Where there is already a binding-up, and qi gathers there, and fluids are retained there, and evil qi strikes into it, the congealing binds tighter day by day, connecting and accumulating in one place, this becomes a xiliu, a hardened, longstanding nodular swelling, which feels firm when pressed with the hand. Where there is a binding-up that penetrates deeply into the bone, so that qi becomes bound up with the bone, and bone and qi merge together, growing larger day by day, this becomes bone-welling (guju, a bone abscess). Where there is a binding-up that strikes into the flesh, and the ancestral qi gathers there, and the evil qi lingers without departing — if there is heat, it transforms into pus; if there is no heat, it becomes a flesh-welling (rouju, a flesh abscess). Now, all these several conditions of qi arise in no fixed place in the body, yet each has its own fixed name.

title_en: Discourse on the Circulation of Defensive Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: "I wish to hear how the defensive qi circulates, and how its emergence and entry correspond to the body — how does this work?" Qibo replied: "The year has twelve months, and the day has twelve double-hours; zi and wu form the warp, and mao and you form the weft. Heaven's circuit comprises twenty-eight lunar lodges, seven stars to each of the four quarters, four sevens making twenty-eight stars in all. Fang and Mao form the weft, while Xu and Zhang form the warp. Thus, from the lodge Fang to the lodge Bi is yang, and from the lodge Mao to the lodge Xin is yin; yang governs the daytime, and yin governs the night. Therefore the circulation of the defensive qi, over the course of one day and one night, makes fifty circuits of the body: during the daytime it travels through the yang for twenty-five circuits, and during the night it travels through the yin for twenty-five circuits, circulating through the five viscera."

Thus, at dawn, when yin is exhausted, the yang qi emerges from the eyes; when the eyes open, the qi ascends to the head, follows the nape, and descends along the Foot Taiyang channel, following the back down to the tip of the little toe. A branch of it separates at the outer canthus of the eye and descends along the Hand Taiyang channel, down to the outer side, between the little finger and the hand. Another branch separates at the outer canthus and descends along the Foot Shaoyang channel, pouring into the space between the fourth and little toes. It then ascends and follows the lateral aspect of the Hand Shaoyang channel, descending to the space between the fingers. A separate branch ascends to a point in front of the ear, joins with the mandibular vessel, and pours into the Foot Yangming channel, descending to the instep and entering the space between the toes. A branch of it descends from below the ear along the Hand Yangming channel, entering the space between the thumb and index finger, and into the palm. When the qi reaches the foot, it enters the sole, emerges at the medial malleolus, travels downward through the yin portion of the leg, and once again joins with the eyes — thus completing one circuit.

Thus, when the sun travels one lodge, the human qi makes one circuit of the body plus eight-tenths of a circuit; when the sun travels two lodges, the human qi makes three circuits of the body plus six-tenths; when the sun travels three lodges, the human qi makes five circuits of the body plus four-tenths; when the sun travels four lodges, the human qi makes seven circuits of the body plus two-tenths; when the sun travels five lodges, the human qi makes nine circuits of the body; when the sun travels six lodges, the human qi makes ten circuits of the body plus eight-tenths; when the sun travels seven lodges, the human qi makes twelve circuits of the body plus six-tenths; when the sun travels fourteen lodges, the human qi makes twenty-five circuits of the body with a remaining fraction of two-tenths — at this point yang is exhausted and passes into yin, and yin then receives the qi. When the qi first enters yin, it always proceeds from the Foot Shaoyin channel and pours into the kidney; the kidney pours into the heart, the heart pours into the lung, the lung pours into the liver, the liver pours into the spleen, and the spleen once again pours into the kidney, completing one circuit. Thus, in the course of the night, for each lodge the sun would travel, the human qi makes one circuit through the yin viscera plus eight-tenths of a circuit, just as in its daytime passage through yang it makes twenty-five circuits, and then once again joins with the eyes. Combining the yang and yin circuits of a single day and night, there remains a fraction of four-tenths of a bodily circuit and two-tenths of a visceral circuit; this is the reason that the time at which people fall asleep and awaken varies, being sometimes early and sometimes late — because this remaining fraction never divides evenly.

Huangdi said: "Since the defensive qi, as it moves through the body, travels up and down without a fixed schedule, how does one wait for the proper moment of qi in order to needle it?" Bogao replied: "The divisions of day and night have their greater and lesser portions, and the days have their longer and shorter lengths; in spring, autumn, winter, and summer, each has its own proportional pattern. Nevertheless, one should always take dawn as the starting point of reckoning, and the end of night as the beginning. Thus, over the course of one day and one night, the water clock drips down a hundred markings (ke); twenty-five markings constitute the measure of half a day. This pattern holds constantly without exception, ending when the sun sets; one reckons according to the varying length of the day and needles accordingly. If one carefully observes the proper timing, the disease can be anticipated and treated in step with it; but if one misses the timing and goes against the proper observation, the hundred diseases cannot be cured. Hence it is said: in needling excess, one needles as the qi is arriving; in needling deficiency, one needles as the qi is departing. This refers to the timing of the qi's presence or absence, by which one observes deficiency and excess and needles accordingly. Thus one must carefully observe where the qi is located and needle it there — this is called 'meeting the proper time.' When the disease is in the three yang channels, one must wait until the qi is in the yang before needling; when the disease is in the three yin channels, one must wait until the qi is in the yin portion before needling."

When the water clock has dripped down one marking, the human qi is in Taiyang; at two markings, the human qi is in Shaoyang; at three markings, the human qi is in Yangming; at four markings, the human qi is in the yin portion. At five markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at six markings, in Shaoyang; at seven markings, in Yangming; at eight markings, in the yin portion. At nine markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at ten markings, in Shaoyang; at eleven markings, in Yangming; at twelve markings, in the yin portion. At thirteen markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at fourteen markings, in Shaoyang; at fifteen markings, in Yangming; at sixteen markings, in the yin portion. At seventeen markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at eighteen markings, in Shaoyang; at nineteen markings, in Yangming; at twenty markings, in the yin portion. At twenty-one markings, the human qi is in Taiyang; at twenty-two markings, in Shaoyang; at twenty-three markings, in Yangming; at twenty-four markings, in the yin portion. At twenty-five markings, the human qi is in Taiyang — this is the measure of half a day. From the lodge Fang to the lodge Bi is fourteen lodges, corresponding to fifty markings of the water clock, during which the sun travels half of its circuit, returning through one lodge for every three markings and four-sevenths of a marking. The essential rule states: whenever the sun arrives at a given lodge, the human qi is in Taiyang; thus, as the sun travels one lodge, the human qi travels through the three yang channels and the yin portion. This pattern continues constantly without ever ceasing; Heaven and Earth share the same reckoning, moving on in endless succession, ending only to begin again — over the course of one day and one night, the water clock drips down a hundred markings, and the cycle is complete.
